message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: ADORE's actual method is a 5-agent hub-and-spoke pipeline whose central idea — 'memory-locked synthesis' — is a HARDER span-grounding constraint than POLARIS already has. ADORE is therefore the wrong system to copy for an insight problem; its own paper contains no ablations and does not name its LLM.
DETAIL: ADORE = Atlassian (You, Sun, Bora, Li, Goel, Li, Culatana; arXiv 2601.18267, 26 Jan 2026, SIGIR'26 submission). Agents: Orchestrator (routes fast-RAG vs deep-research by 'research complexity'); Grounding (clarification questions to fix underspecified prompts); Planning (sections/priorities/success criteria as a user-editable shared artifact); Execution (Self-Evolution Engine evolves queries from detected evidence gaps); Report Generation (initial outline → outline revision on retrieved evidence → final report); WebSearch. Three contributions: (1) Memory-locked synthesis — 'each section is written using ONLY the section-scoped evidence stored in the claim–evidence graph... traceability by construction'; (2) Evidence-coverage-guided execution — section-level coverage audits drive targeted re-retrieval, stopping on coverage+citation-stability rather than a fixed iteration budget; (3) Section-packed long-context grounding — pack only section-relevant evidence per generation call, prune low-salience text, compress sources into citation-preserving summaries. CRITICAL READ: (1) is a STRICTER admissible-evidence constraint than POLARIS's span-grounding, and RACE strips citations anyway, so ADORE's headline contribution buys POLARIS nothing on RACE. Its real, transferable content is (2)+(3), which are re-derivations of WebWeaver. The paper has NO ablation table, names no backbone model, and its RACE numbers (52.65 / comp 52.22 / insight 54.37 / IF 51.11 / read 52.18) are self-reported under the legacy judge.
SYSTEM: ADORE
SOURCE GIVEN: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.18267v1
STATED CONFIDENCE: high

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt: What is the title, authors, and abstract of this paper?

input.query: ADORE Atlassian deep research agent arXiv 2601.18267

content.content:
# Paper Summary

**Title:** "Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG"

**Authors:** Xincheng You, Qi Sun, Neha Bora, Huayi Li, Shubham Goel, Kang Li, and Sean Culatana

**Abstract:**

The researchers present ADORE, a framework designed to improve Retrieval-Augmented Generation for enterprise applications. Traditional RAG systems often produce shallow results lacking proper traceability in high-stakes scenarios. Their solution replaces linear retrieval with iterative investigation guided by specialized agents and a central coordinator.

The framework's key innovations include: (1) constraining report generation to a structured Memory Bank with claim-evidence connections for verifiable citations; (2) implementing a retrieval-reflection loop that audits evidence coverage and determines when to stop searching; and (3) managing long-form synthesis within context limits through section-level organization and compression.

The system achieved top rankings on evaluation benchmarks, scoring 52.65 on DeepResearch Bench and winning 77.2% of head-to-head comparisons against commercial alternatives.

Web search results for query: "ADORE Atlassian deep research agent arXiv 2601.18267"

Links: [{"title":"Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG Xincheng You","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.18267"},{"title":"[2601.18267] Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18267"},{"title":"[2506.18096] Deep Research Agents: A Systematic Examination And Roadmap","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.18096"},{"title":"[2601.06676] One Interaction Is Worth a Thousand Guesses: Benchmarking the Interactive Capabilities of Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.06676"},{"title":"MiroEval: Benchmarking Multimodal Deep Research Agents in Process and Outcome","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.28407"},{"title":"MetaResearcher: Scaling Deep Research via Self-Reflective Reinforcement Learning in Adversarial Virtual Environments","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.19893"},{"title":"GitHub - ai-agents-2030/awesome-deep-research-agent · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/ai-agents-2030/awesome-deep-research-agent"},{"title":"Inference-Time Scaling of Verification: Self-Evolving Deep Research Agents via Test-Time Rubric-Guided Verification","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.15808"},{"title":"How Far Are We from Genuinely Useful Deep Research Agents?","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.01948"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about ADORE and arXiv paper 2601.18267:

## Overview

ADORE (Adaptive Deep Orchestration for Research in Enterprise) is an agentic framework that replaces linear retrieval with iterative, user-steered investigation coordinated by a central orchestrator and a set of specialized agents.

## Problem It Addresses

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) shows promise for enterprise knowledge work, yet it often underperforms in high-stakes decision settings that require deep synthesis, strict traceability, and recovery from underspecified prompts. One-pass retrieval-and-write pipelines frequently yield shallow summaries, inconsistent grounding, and weak mechanisms for completeness verification.

## Key Innovation

ADORE's key insight is that a structured Memory Bank (a curated evidence store with explicit claim-evidence linkage and section-level admissible evidence) enables traceable report generation and systematic checks for evidence completeness.

## Differentiation from Other Approaches

ADORE is inspired by recent deep-research systems, but it differs in what drives iteration and what constitutes convergence. ADORE introduces Memory-locked synthesis: it treats the Memory Bank as a hard constraint on generation (section-scoped admissible evidence with explicit claim–evidence linkage), then uses section-level evidence coverage audits to diagnose gaps and drive targeted follow-up retrieval with an evidence-driven stopping rule.

## Paper Details

The paper was submitted on 26 Jan 2026. The authors include Xincheng You, Qi Sun, Neha Bora, and others from Atlassian.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.prompt: List: (a) the exact agents in the architecture and whether it's hub-and-spoke; (b) the three contributions verbatim-ish, especially "memory-locked synthesis" wording about section-scoped evidence only; (c) any mention of a Self-Evolution Engine; (d) the exact DeepResearch Bench RACE scores (overall, comprehensiveness, insight, instruction-following, readability); (e) which LLM/backbone model is used (name it if stated anywhere, including appendix/implementation details); (f) is there an ablation study or ablation table? (g) which judge model was used for RACE evaluation (e.g. Gemini-2.5-Pro or GPT-5.5) and whether scores are self-reported or from the official leaderboard.

content.content:
# ADORE Framework Analysis

## (a) Agents and Architecture

**Agents listed:**
- Orchestrator Agent (central hub)
- Grounding Agent
- Planning Agent
- Execution Agent
- Report Generation Agent
- WebSearch Agent

**Architecture:** Yes, explicitly hub-and-spoke. The paper states: "hub-and-spoke architecture governed by a central Orchestrator Agent."

---

## (b) Three Contributions (Verbatim)

1. **Memory-locked synthesis:** "report generation is constrained to a structured Memory Bank (Claim–Evidence Graph with section-level admissible evidence), ensuring that claims are traceable and citations are grounded in retrieved sources."

2. **Evidence-coverage–guided execution:** "use section-level evidence coverage audits to localize weak or missing sections, trigger targeted follow-up retrieval, and terminate via an evidence-driven stopping criterion."

3. **Section-packed long-context grounding:** "packing section-specific evidence, pruning redundant context, and compressing retrieved content into citation-preserving summaries so synthesis stays within context limits."

---

## (c) Self-Evolution Engine

Yes, mentioned explicitly. The Execution Agent "uses a Self-Evolution Engine to evolve search queries based on intermediate findings and detected evidence gaps, enabling multi-hop recovery."

---

## (d) DeepResearch Bench RACE Scores

- **Overall:** 52.65
- **Comprehensiveness:** 52.22
- **Insight:** 54.37
- **Instruction Following:** 51.11
- **Readability:** 52.18

---

## (e) LLM/Backbone Model

**Not specified.** The paper contains no explicit naming of the underlying LLM architecture or model used to power ADORE.

---

## (f) Ablation Study

**No ablation table or study is present** in this document.

---

## (g) Judge Model for RACE & Score Source

**Judge model not disclosed.** The paper states RACE "generates a prompt-specific weight vector" but does not name the judge model. Scores are self-reported; the document does not indicate whether they come from an official leaderboard.

input.command:
cd /tmp && curl -sL https://arxiv.org/html/2601.18267v1 -o adore.html && wc -c adore.html && python3 -c "
import re,html
t=open('adore.html',encoding='utf-8').read()
t=re.sub(r'<[^>]+>',' ',t); t=html.unescape(t); t=re.sub(r'\s+',' ',t)
open('adore.txt','w').write(t)
print(len(t))
for kw in ['Gemini','GPT','Claude','judge','Judge','backbone','ablation','Ablation','leaderboard','Memory-locked','memory-locked','traceability by construction','only the','5.5','2.5 Pro','2.5-Pro','LLM']:
    for m in re.finditer(re.escape(kw),t):
        print('---',kw,':',t[max(0,m.start()-160):m.start()+200])
" | head -120

input.query: DeepResearch Bench RACE judge model GPT-5.5 replaced Gemini-2.5-Pro leaderboard 2026

input.prompt: What judge model does RACE use? Has it changed from Gemini-2.5-Pro to GPT-5.5 in 2026? What is the current leaderboard and top scores? Is ADORE or Tavily Research listed?

content.content:
# DeepResearch Bench Judge Model Update

**RACE Judge Model Change:**
Yes, the judge model has changed. Originally using Gemini-2.5-Pro, the benchmark switched to **GPT-5.5** as its official evaluator in May 2026. According to the documentation, "GPT-5.5 wins on Overall, PAR, and FAS" metrics when compared against competing models.

**Current Evaluation Setup:**
The system now uses "openai/gpt-5.5" for RACE evaluation and "openai/gpt-5.4-mini" for FACT evaluation when using OpenRouter as the backend.

**Leaderboard Information:**
The page directs users to the "DeepResearch Bench Leaderboard" hosted on Hugging Face for current rankings and scores, but specific leaderboard positions and top scores are not displayed in this GitHub repository content.

**ADORE and Tavily Research:**
Neither ADORE nor Tavily Research are mentioned anywhere in the provided documentation or evaluation results.

Web search results for query: "DeepResearch Bench RACE judge model GPT-5.5 replaced Gemini-2.5-Pro leaderboard 2026"

Links: [{"title":"Gemini 2.5 Pro vs GPT-5.5 Pro: AI Benchmark Comparison 2026 | BenchLM.ai","url":"https://benchlm.ai/compare/gemini-2-5-pro-vs-gpt-5-5-pro"},{"title":"GitHub - Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench: DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.11763"},{"title":"AI Model Leaderboard 2026: GPT-5, Claude, Gemini ...","url":"https://localaimaster.com/tools/ai-model-leaderboard"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/"},{"title":"LLM Benchmark Leaderboard 2026: Coding, Math, Reasoning and Agents | CodeSOTA | CodeSOTA","url":"https://www.codesota.com/llm"},{"title":"SWE-rebench Leaderboard","url":"https://swe-rebench.com/"},{"title":"RhinoInsight: Improving Deep Research through Control Mechanisms for Model Behavior and Context","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2511.18743"},{"title":"FinDeepResearch: Evaluating Deep Research Agents in Rigorous Financial Analysis","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.13936"},{"title":"ShoppingComp: Are LLMs Really Ready for Your Shopping Cart?","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2511.22978"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the DeepResearch Bench RACE judge model transition:

Following Google's announced June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro, DeepResearch Bench benchmarked three frontier reasoning models as candidate replacements on the human-annotated subset (50 tasks × 4 target DRAs = 200 articles), measuring each candidate's alignment with human judgments (human inter-annotator agreement baseline = 68.78%).

All three candidates exceeded this baseline by 1.3–3 points; GPT-5.5 won on Overall, PAR, and FAS, and was adopted as the new RACE evaluator (with GPT-5.4-mini for the FACT pipeline).

From now through May 31, 2026, DeepResearch Bench accepted submissions evaluated under both the legacy evaluator (Gemini-2.5-Pro) and the new one (GPT-5.5), with results displayed on two separate leaderboards so the rankings remained directly comparable within each evaluator.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.prompt: List the current RACE leaderboard entries and scores. Which judge model? Is ADORE listed? Is there a legacy (Gemini-2.5-Pro) vs new (GPT-5.5) leaderboard split?

content.content:
Based on the provided content, I can only answer part of your question:

**Judge Model:** "Gemini 2.5 Pro Preview" was selected as the Judge LLM, achieving "the best overall performance score of 72.56%."

**RACE Leaderboard Entries:** The document presents results from the initial evaluation but does not display a current leaderboard. The main results table includes four Deep Research Agents (Grok Deeper Search, Perplexity Deep Research, Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research, and OpenAI Deep Research) with their RACE scores.

**ADORE, Legacy vs. New Split:** Neither ADORE nor any model version splits (legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro versus GPT-5.5) are mentioned in this webpage content.

To access current leaderboard information, you would need to visit the actual leaderboard link referenced: "https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard"

content.text:
All core factual assertions check out against the primary source. Details.

**Primary source: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.18267v1 (fetched full text, 2601.18267v1)**

| Sub-claim | Status |
|---|---|
| Title/authors/venue | CONFIRMED. "Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG" — You, Sun, Bora, Li, Goel, Li, Culatana (Atlassian), submitted 26 Jan 2026. ACM template metadata: "Proceedings of the 49th International ACM SIGIR Conference…; July 21–25, 2026" — i.e. formatted as a SIGIR'26 submission, **not** confirmed accepted. |
| Hub-and-spoke | CONFIRMED verbatim: "ADORE abandons a single linear pipeline in favor of a hub-and-spoke architecture governed by a central Orchestrator Agent." |
| Agent roster | CONFIRMED: Orchestrator + Grounding, Planning, Execution, Report Generation, WebSearch. (Pedantic note: that's 5 spokes + 1 hub = 6 agents; "5-agent" undercounts unless you mean 5 specialists.) |
| Self-Evolution Engine | CONFIRMED — Execution Agent "uses a Self-Evolution Engine to evolve search queries based on intermediate findings and detected evidence gaps." |
| Memory-locked synthesis quote | CONFIRMED verbatim (§2.4.2): "each section is written using **only** the section-scoped evidence stored in the claim–evidence graph. This enables **traceability by construction** … and supports citation auditing by verifying that cited sources and **quoted spans** appear in the admissible evidence." |
| Contributions (2) and (3) | CONFIRMED as stated (coverage audits + evidence-driven stopping on coverage & citation stability; section-packing, pruning, citation-preserving compression). |
| **No ablation** | CONFIRMED. Zero occurrences of "ablat"/"Ablat" in the full text. |
| **No LLM named** | CONFIRMED. Zero occurrences of "backbone", "base model", or any implementation-details section naming a model. The only model names in the paper are baselines/competitors. |
| RACE numbers | CONFIRMED exactly: ADORE 52.65 / Comp 52.22 / Insight 54.37 / Inst 51.11 / Read 52.18, Table 2, rank 1 above Tavily Research 52.44 and ThinkDepthAI 52.43. |

**Currency / staleness (the part you asked me to press on):** the claim's "legacy judge" framing is right, and it matters more than the claim says.
- The paper never names the RACE judge — it just cites Du et al. 2025. Since it was submitted 26 Jan 2026 and DeepResearch Bench only swapped evaluators later (Gemini-2.5-Pro deprecation announced 17 Jun 2026; GPT-5.5 adopted as the RACE evaluator, GPT-5.4-mini for FACT; the repo's current config is `openai/gpt-5.5`), ADORE's 52.65 is necessarily under the legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro evaluator. Confirmed via https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench.
- I could **not** confirm ADORE appears on the official HF leaderboard at all (the Space is JS-rendered / 401'd; the repo README mentions neither ADORE nor Tavily Research). So "self-reported" is the correct characterization, and the paper's "rank #1 on the leaderboard (Du et al., 2025)" is an unverified self-placement on a leaderboard configuration that no longer exists.

**Two parts of the claim that are interpretation, not source-verified — do not present them as facts:**
1. *"(1) is a STRICTER constraint than POLARIS's span-grounding … and RACE strips citations anyway, so it buys POLARIS nothing on RACE."* The stricter-than-POLARIS half is an internal comparison I cannot check from any primary source. The "RACE strips citations" half is a claim about RACE, not about ADORE, and I did not verify it from the RACE paper/code in this pass — RACE is the 4-dimension quality rubric and FACT is the separate citation metric, which makes the claim plausible, but it is currently unsourced.
2. *"(2)+(3) are re-derivations of WebWeaver."* Editorial. The paper itself concedes proximity ("WebWeaver emphasizes outline-centric evidence structuring and section-level writing with a memory bank") and positions ADORE as differing in "what drives iteration and what constitutes convergence" — but it does not concede re-derivation, and with no ablation there is nothing in the paper that isolates (2)/(3)'s contribution either way.

Bottom line: the load-bearing, checkable facts (architecture, memory-locked synthesis wording, no ablation, no named backbone, the five RACE numbers, legacy-judge staleness) are all confirmed from the primary source. The strategic conclusion ("wrong system to copy") rests on the two interpretive legs above, one of which (RACE ignoring citations) still needs a primary-source check before it carries weight.

input.reasoning:
Verified against the primary source (full text of https://arxiv.org/html/2601.18267v1, fetched and grepped) plus the DeepResearch Bench repo.

CONFIRMED from primary source:
- Paper: "Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG", You/Sun/Bora/Li/Goel/Li/Culatana (Atlassian), arXiv 2601.18267, submitted 26 Jan 2026. ACM metadata targets the 49th ACM SIGIR (July 21-25, 2026), so "SIGIR'26 submission" is right; acceptance is NOT established.
- Architecture verbatim: "ADORE abandons a single linear pipeline in favor of a hub-and-spoke architecture governed by a central Orchestrator Agent." Agents: Orchestrator (hub) + Grounding, Planning, Execution, Report Generation, WebSearch. (Minor: that is 5 spokes + 1 hub = 6 agents; "5-agent" is only right if it means 5 specialists.)
- Execution Agent "uses a Self-Evolution Engine to evolve search queries based on intermediate findings and detected evidence gaps" - confirmed.
- Memory-locked synthesis, section 2.4.2, verbatim: "each section is written using only the section-scoped evidence stored in the claim-evidence graph. This enables traceability by construction ... and supports citation auditing by verifying that cited sources and quoted spans appear in the admissible evidence." Exactly as claimed.
- Contributions (2) evidence-coverage-guided execution with an evidence-driven stopping criterion (coverage + citation stability) and (3) section-packed long-context grounding (packing, pruning, citation-preserving compression) - confirmed as stated.
- NO ABLATION: zero occurrences of "ablat"/"Ablat" in the full text. Confirmed.
- NO NAMED LLM: zero occurrences of "backbone", "base model", or any implementation/model-config section. The only model names in the paper are baselines/competitors. Confirmed.
- RACE numbers exact: ADORE 52.65 / Comp 52.22 / Insight 54.37 / Inst 51.11 / Read 52.18 (Table 2, rank 1 above Tavily Research 52.44 and ThinkDepthAI 52.43). Confirmed.

CURRENCY: the claim's "legacy judge" framing is correct and understated. The paper never names the RACE judge; it just cites Du et al. 2025. Because it was submitted 26 Jan 2026 and DeepResearch Bench only swapped evaluators later (Gemini-2.5-Pro deprecation announced 17 Jun 2026; GPT-5.5 adopted as the RACE evaluator, GPT-5.4-mini for FACT; the repo config now reads openai/gpt-5.5), the 52.65 is necessarily under the legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro evaluator and is stale w.r.t. the current leaderboard. I could not confirm ADORE appears on the official HF leaderboard at all (Space is JS-rendered/401; repo README mentions neither ADORE nor Tavily Research), so "self-reported" is the correct characterization and the paper's "rank #1 on the leaderboard" is an unverified self-placement on a configuration that no longer exists.

TWO PARTS ARE INTERPRETATION, NOT SOURCE-VERIFIED (do not present as fact):
1. "(1) is a STRICTER constraint than POLARIS's span-grounding, and RACE strips citations anyway, so it buys POLARIS nothing on RACE." The stricter-than-POLARIS half is an internal comparison unverifiable from any primary source. The "RACE strips citations" half is a claim about RACE, not ADORE, and I did NOT verify it from the RACE paper/code in this pass. It is plausible (RACE is the 4-dimension quality rubric; FACT is the separate citation metric) but currently unsourced - check it before leaning on it.
2. "(2)+(3) are re-derivations of WebWeaver." Editorial. The paper concedes proximity ("WebWeaver emphasizes outline-centric evidence structuring and section-level writing with a memory bank") and claims it differs in "what drives iteration and what constitutes convergence" - but with no ablation, nothing in the paper isolates (2)/(3) either way.

Verdict CONFIRMED for every checkable factual assertion. The strategic conclusion ("wrong system to copy") rests on the two interpretive legs above.
input.corrected: No factual correction needed. Two precision notes: (a) the architecture is 6 agents (central Orchestrator hub + 5 spokes: Grounding, Planning, Execution, Report Generation, WebSearch), not 5, unless "5-agent" is meant as "5 specialists around a hub"; (b) the paper is formatted for SIGIR'26 (49th ACM SIGIR, July 21-25 2026) but there is no evidence of acceptance - call it a preprint/submission. Also: ADORE's paper does not itself name the RACE judge; the "legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro judge" attribution is a sound inference from the 26 Jan 2026 submission date plus DeepResearch Bench's mid-2026 switch to GPT-5.5 (source: https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench), not a statement in the paper.
